explanation:
GitOps uses Git as the single source of truth for infrastructure and application state. Desired state is declared in Git; a controller reconciles actual state with desired state. Changes happen through PRs, not manual commands.

Four principles: declarative, versioned/immutable (Git), automatically applied (pulled), continuously reconciled (drift detection).
example:
```yaml
# k8s/deployment.yaml
apiVersion: apps/v1
kind: Deployment
metadata:
  name: my-app
  namespace: production
spec:
  replicas: 3
  selector:
    matchLabels:
      app: my-app
  template:
    metadata:
      labels:
        app: my-app
    spec:
      containers:
      - name: my-app
        image: myregistry.io/my-app:v1.2.3
        ports:
        - containerPort: 8080
        resources:
          requests:
            cpu: 100m
            memory: 128Mi
          limits:
            cpu: 500m
            memory: 256Mi
```

```bash
# GitOps workflow:
# 1. Dev pushes code -> CI builds image with new tag
# 2. CI updates image tag in k8s config Git repo
# 3. ArgoCD detects change in Git
# 4. ArgoCD applies change to cluster

# Update image tag in CI
sed -i 's|myregistry.io/my-app:.*|myregistry.io/my-app:v1.2.4|' production/deployment.yaml
git commit -am 'chore: update my-app to v1.2.4'
git push
```
key_takeaway: Git is the single source of truth. Changes through PRs, not kubectl. Controller continuously reconciles actual with desired state.
try_it: Create a Git repo with K8s manifests. Make a change via PR and verify it gets applied.

explanation: ArgoCD is a declarative GitOps controller for Kubernetes. It watches Git repos and syncs to clusters. Applications map Git repos to cluster namespaces. The UI shows sync status, health, and history.
example:
```bash
# Install ArgoCD
kubectl create namespace argocd
kubectl apply -n argocd -f https://raw.githubusercontent.com/argoproj/argo-cd/stable/manifests/install.yaml

# Get initial password
argocd admin initial-password -n argocd

# Port forward and login
kubectl port-forward svc/argocd-server -n argocd 8080:443
argocd login localhost:8080

# Register cluster
argocd cluster add my-cluster
```

```yaml
# ArgoCD Application
apiVersion: argoproj.io/v1alpha1
kind: Application
metadata:
  name: my-app
  namespace: argocd
spec:
  project: default
  source:
    repoURL: https://github.com/org/k8s-configs.git
    targetRevision: main
    path: production
  destination:
    server: https://kubernetes.default.svc
    namespace: production
  syncPolicy:
    automated:
      prune: true
      selfHeal: true
    syncOptions:
      - CreateNamespace=true
    retry:
      limit: 5
      backoff:
        duration: 5s
        factor: 2
        maxDuration: 3m
```

```bash
# CLI
argocd app create my-app \
  --repo https://github.com/org/k8s-configs.git \
  --path production \
  --dest-server https://kubernetes.default.svc \
  --dest-namespace production \
  --sync-policy automated --auto-prune --self-heal

argocd app sync my-app
argocd app get my-app
argocd app history my-app
```
key_takeaway: ArgoCD watches Git and syncs to clusters. Use automated sync with prune and selfHeal. Register clusters and create applications.
try_it: Install ArgoCD, register your cluster, create an application, verify automatic sync.

explanation: Sync status shows if Git matches cluster. Health status shows if resources are healthy. Automated sync applies changes automatically. Manual sync gives control. Rollback reverts to previous revision.
example:
```bash
# Check status
argocd app get my-app
# Sync Status: Synced
# Health Status: Healthy

# View diff
argocd app diff my-app

# Sync history
argocd app history my-app

# Rollback
argocd app rollback my-app 1

# Refresh from Git
argocd app get my-app --refresh
```

```yaml
# Ignore HPA-controlled replicas
apiVersion: argoproj.io/v1alpha1
kind: Application
metadata:
  name: my-app
spec:
  ignoreDifferences:
  - group: apps
    kind: Deployment
    jsonPointers:
    - /spec/replicas
```
key_takeaway: Synced + Healthy = desired matches reality. Use diff to preview, rollback to revert, ignoreDifferences for HPA fields.
try_it: Make a manual kubectl change, observe the drift, then sync to fix it.

explanation: ArgoCD manages apps across multiple clusters from one control plane. Register clusters, create projects for isolation, deploy to specific clusters. Enables multi-environment (dev/staging/prod) and multi-region.
example:
```bash
# Register clusters
argocd cluster add staging-context --name staging
argocd cluster add prod-context --name production
argocd cluster list

# Create project
argocd proj create my-project \
  --src https://github.com/org/k8s-configs.git \
  --dest https://staging-cluster,production \
  --dest https://prod-cluster,production
```

```yaml
# Project with RBAC
apiVersion: argoproj.io/v1alpha1
kind: AppProject
metadata:
  name: my-project
  namespace: argocd
spec:
  sourceRepos:
  - 'https://github.com/org/k8s-configs.git'
  destinations:
  - server: https://staging-cluster
    namespace: production
  - server: https://prod-cluster
    namespace: production
  roles:
  - name: developer
    policies:
    - p, proj:my-project:developer, applications, get, my-project/*, allow
    - p, proj:my-project:developer, applications, sync, my-project/*, allow
```

```bash
# Create app targeting specific cluster
argocd app create staging-app \
  --repo https://github.com/org/k8s-configs.git \
  --path staging --dest-server https://staging-cluster --dest-namespace production

# Promote
argocd app set prod-app --revision main
argocd app sync prod-app
```
key_takeaway: Register clusters, create projects for RBAC isolation, target specific clusters per app. Promote by updating target revision.
try_it: Register two clusters, create a project, deploy the same app to both with different configs.

explanation: Drift detection finds differences between Git (desired) and cluster (actual). Auto-sync corrects drift automatically. Self-heal re-applies if someone manually changes the cluster. Notifications alert on failures.
example:
```yaml
apiVersion: argoproj.io/v1alpha1
kind: Application
metadata:
  name: my-app
  namespace: argocd
spec:
  source:
    repoURL: https://github.com/org/k8s-configs.git
    targetRevision: main
    path: production
  destination:
    server: https://kubernetes.default.svc
    namespace: production
  syncPolicy:
    automated:
      prune: true        # delete resources removed from Git
      selfHeal: true      # revert manual changes
    syncOptions:
      - CreateNamespace=true
      - PruneLast=true
      - ApplyOutOfSyncOnly=true
    retry:
      limit: 5
      backoff:
        duration: 5s
        factor: 2
        maxDuration: 3m
```

```bash
# Check drift
argocd app diff my-app

# View sync history
argocd app get my-app --show-operation

# Manual correction
argocd app sync my-app
```
key_takeaway: Auto-sync with selfHeal prevents drift. Prune removes deleted resources. Notifications alert on sync failures.
try_it: Enable auto-sync with selfHeal. Manually scale a deployment with kubectl. Verify ArgoCD reverts it.

explanation:
GitOps problems revolve around declarative infrastructure management and continuous reconciliation. The key patterns are: defining desired state in Git, automating sync with ArgoCD or Flux, detecting and remediating drift, and managing multi-environment promotions.

Focus on understanding the reconciliation loop: Git is the source of truth, the controller compares actual cluster state to desired state, and automatically corrects any drift. This eliminates manual kubectl apply and ensures every change is auditable through Git history.
example:
```yaml
# ArgoCD Application manifest
apiVersion: argoproj.io/v1alpha1
kind: Application
metadata:
  name: my-app
  namespace: argocd
spec:
  project: default
  source:
    repoURL: https://github.com/org/k8s-configs.git
    targetRevision: main
    path: overlays/production
  destination:
    server: https://kubernetes.default.svc
    namespace: production
  syncPolicy:
    automated:
      prune: true
      selfHeal: true
    syncOptions:
      - CreateNamespace=true
```
key_takeaway: GitOps means Git is the single source of truth — every infrastructure change goes through a PR, and a controller continuously reconciles actual state with desired state.
try_it: Set up an ArgoCD Application that watches a Git repo path and auto-syncs when the manifest changes.

practice_challenges.title: ArgoCD Application with Auto-Sync
practice_challenges.hint: Define an ArgoCD Application resource with syncPolicy.automated enabled.
practice_challenges.description: Create an ArgoCD Application manifest that watches a Git repo path and automatically deploys changes to a Kubernetes namespace.
practice_challenges.example_input: Git repo at github.com/org/k8s-configs, path: apps/nginx
practice_challenges.example_output: ArgoCD auto-syncs every commit to the cluster

practice_challenges.title: Multi-Environment Promotion Pipeline
practice_challenges.hint: Use Kustomize overlays for dev/staging/prod and promote by updating image tags or merging PRs.
practice_challenges.description: Set up dev, staging, and production overlays using Kustomize. Changes flow: dev (auto) -> staging (auto) -> prod (manual PR).
practice_challenges.example_input: New image tag v1.2.3 pushed to dev overlay
practice_challenges.example_output: v1.2.3 auto-deploys to dev, then staging, then waits for prod PR approval

practice_challenges.title: Drift Detection and Auto-Remediation
practice_challenges.hint: Enable selfHeal in ArgoCD syncPolicy and use diff to detect manual changes.
practice_challenges.description: A team member manually scaled a deployment with kubectl scale. Configure ArgoCD to detect this drift and revert it automatically.
practice_challenges.example_output: ArgoCD detects drift, scales back to 3, logs the event

practice_challenges.title: Secret Management with Sealed Secrets
practice_challenges.hint: Use kubeseal to encrypt secrets that can only be decrypted by the controllers key in the cluster.
practice_challenges.description: Store encrypted database credentials in Git using Sealed Secrets. The sealed secret is safe to commit; only the cluster can decrypt it.
practice_challenges.example_output: SealedSecret YAML safe to commit to Git, decrypted only in-cluster

practice_challenges.title: GitOps Canary Deployment with Argo Rollouts
practice_challenges.hint: Use Argo Rollouts CRD with canary strategy, steps for traffic shifting, and analysis templates for automated rollback.
practice_challenges.description: Implement a canary deployment that sends 10% traffic to the new version, monitors error rates, and auto-rolls back if errors exceed 5%.
practice_challenges.example_input: New version v2.0.0 ready to deploy alongside v1.x
practice_challenges.example_output: Traffic gradually shifted: 10% -> 25% -> 50% -> 100% with health checks at each step
